schools.summary: The warning and the duty of discernment: 'beloved, believe not every spirit (every claimed inspiration or teacher), but try (dokimazete — test, prove, examine) the spirits whether they are of God: because many false prophets are gone out into the world' — believers not to accept every teacher claiming the Spirit but to test them (discernment a Christian duty); 'the spirits' being the teachers claiming to speak by the Spirit, to be tested by a standard (the confession of Christ, v.2), since not all who claim inspiration are truly of God; the proliferation of false teachers ('many false prophets are gone out into the world') the reason testing is needed, the world full of those who falsely claim to speak for God, their multitude demanding vigilant discernment (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: believe not every spirit, but try the spirits whether they are of God
because many false prophets are gone out into the world
the call to test the spirits
discernment a Christian duty

schools.summary: The criterion of true inspiration: 'hereby (by this) know ye the Spirit of God: Every spirit that confesseth (homologei — acknowledges, openly professes) that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh (has truly come as a real man, incarnate) is of God' — John providing a definite criterion by which to distinguish the Spirit of God from false spirits; the true test being the confession of the incarnation (the spirit that confesses Christ truly come in the flesh is of God); the test targeting the early error (the docetists/gnostics) who denied that Christ truly came in real flesh — to confess 'Jesus Christ come in the flesh' being to affirm the genuine incarnation (the true humanity, and the union of the divine Christ with it) against those who denied it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Hereby know ye the Spirit of God: Every spirit that confesseth that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is of God
the test of the Spirit: confessing Christ come in the flesh
the confession of the genuine incarnation
against the docetic denial of Christ's flesh

schools.summary: The converse of v.2: 'and every spirit that confesseth not (denies, refuses to acknowledge) that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is not of God: and this is that spirit of antichrist, whereof ye have heard that it should come; and even now already is it in the world' — the spirit that denies the incarnation not from God (the refusal of this confession marking the false spirit); this denial of the incarnate Christ being the very spirit of antichrist (the antichristian opposition to Christ John had warned of, to deny Christ come in the flesh being antichristian); the antichristian spirit, foretold as coming, already at work (not merely a future figure but a present reality in the false teachers) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: every spirit that confesseth not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is not of God
this is that spirit of antichrist... even now already is it in the world
the spirit of antichrist that denies Christ come in the flesh
the antichristian spirit already at work

schools.summary: The assurance of victory: 'ye are of God (belong to God), little children, and have overcome them (the false prophets, the antichristian spirits): because greater is he that is in you (God, the Holy Spirit, dwelling in the believer), than he that is in the world (the devil, the spirit of antichrist, working in the world)' — the believers, belonging to God, having already overcome the false teachers (victory over the spirit of antichrist theirs); the overcoming not by their own strength but by God's power working in them; the reason for their victory the superior power of the God within them over the evil one in the world (the indwelling God greater than all opposing powers, the believer's confidence resting on this) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Augustine: the believers overcome the false prophets not by their own power but by him who dwells in them; greater is God within than the devil in the world, so the victory is sure — the conquering of error and the antichristian spirit being the work of the indwelling God, not human strength.

themes: ye are of God, little children, and have overcome them
greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world
the believers' victory through the greater One within them
the indwelling God overpowering the spirit of antichrist

schools.summary: The contrast with the believers (v.4): 'they (the false teachers) are of the world (belong to the world, have its nature): therefore speak they of (ek — from, according to) the world, and the world heareth them' — the false teachers belonging to the world, so their teaching worldly in origin and character (speaking from the world's viewpoint, their message suiting the world's wisdom, values, and desires, nothing of God in it but reflecting the world from which it springs); and the world receiving them because they are its own (like attracts like, the worldly gladly hearing a worldly message, the reception of the false teachers by the world itself a mark of their origin) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Cyril: the world hears the false teachers because they are of the world, kindred to it; like is received by like, and the worldly message finds a ready hearing among the worldly — the world's reception of these teachers betraying that they spring from the world, not from God.

themes: They are of the world: therefore speak they of the world, and the world heareth them
the false teachers of the world, heard by the world
the worldly message received by the worldly
like attracting like

schools.summary: The apostolic test: 'we (the apostles) are of God: he that knoweth God heareth (receives, listens to) us; he that is not of God heareth not (rejects) us. Hereby (by this) know we the spirit of truth (the Holy Spirit, true teaching), and the spirit of error (false, deceiving spirit)' — the apostles of God, and the one who truly knows God receiving their teaching (receptiveness to the apostolic message a mark of being of God); the one who does not belong to God rejecting the apostolic teaching (rejection of the apostles' word marking the one not of God, the response to the apostolic message testing the hearer); by this response the two spirits distinguished — reception of the apostolic gospel marking the spirit of truth, rejection the spirit of error (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: We are of God: he that knoweth God heareth us; he that is not of God heareth not us
Hereby know we the spirit of truth, and the spirit of error
the test of hearing the apostles
the response to the apostolic word distinguishing the spirits

schools.summary: The exhortation grounded in love's origin: 'beloved, let us love one another: for love is of God (springs from God, has God as its source); and every one that loveth is born of God, and knoweth God' — believers to love one another because love itself comes from God (love divine in origin, a reflection of God's own nature, all true love originating in God who is its fountain, not merely human sentiment but divine in source); genuine love being the proof of the new birth and of knowing God (the one who loves showing he is God's child and has true acquaintance with God, love the mark of regeneration and of the knowledge of God) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: let us love one another: for love is of God
every one that loveth is born of God, and knoweth God
love is of God, and the one who loves is born of God
love divine in source and the mark of the new birth

schools.summary: The converse and the supreme declaration: 'he that loveth not knoweth not (has no true knowledge of) God; for God is love' — the absence of love proving the absence of the knowledge of God (one who does not love cannot truly know God, because the very nature of God is love); lovelessness decisive evidence of not knowing God (since God is love, to be without love is to be without acquaintance with God, a loveless person, whatever his profession, not knowing God); 'God is love' the sublime statement that love is the very essence and nature of God — not merely that God loves, but that God IS love, love being what God is, so that to know God is to love, and to love is to share God's nature (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Augustine: 'God is love' — love is not merely something God has or does but what God is in his very being; therefore the one who dwells in love dwells in God, and the one without love is without God, however he may profess to know him, for to be estranged from love is to be estranged from God himself.

themes: He that loveth not knoweth not God; for God is love
the loveless do not know God, for God is love
love the very nature and essence of God
lovelessness proving ignorance of God

schools.summary: The supreme display of God's love: 'in this (by this act) was manifested (made visible, shown) the love of God toward us, because that God sent his only begotten (monogenes — unique, only) Son into the world, that we might live (have spiritual and eternal life) through him' — God's love supremely revealed in a definite act (the sending of his Son the visible manifestation of the love that God is, v.8); the love shown in the greatness of the gift (not a created being but his own only Son, sent into the world, the costliness of the gift measuring the love); the purpose of the sending being that we, dead in sin, might live through Christ (the love aimed at giving life to the spiritually dead, life through the Son the goal of the love) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: In this was manifested the love of God toward us, because that God sent his only begotten Son into the world
that we might live through him
the love of God manifested in sending his Son that we might live
the costly gift of the Son giving life to the dead

schools.summary: The definition of love: 'herein is love, not that we (first) loved God, but that he loved us, and sent his Son to be the propitiation (hilasmos — the atoning sacrifice that turns away wrath and covers sin) for our sins' — true love defined not by our love for God but by his prior love for us (God's love original, not a response to ours, he loving us first when we did not love him); God's love preceding and causing ours (we did not earn or initiate it, his love for unworthy, unloving sinners the pattern and source of all love, love beginning with God); the proof of his love being the sending of his Son as the propitiation (Christ's atoning sacrifice for our sins the supreme expression of the love that loved us first, love shown in the cross that deals with sin) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: Herein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us
sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins
love defined by God's love for us in the propitiation
God's prior, original love proven in the atonement

schools.summary: The duty drawn from God's love: 'beloved, if God so loved us (so greatly, at such cost, in sending his Son), we ought also (are bound, opheilomen) to love one another' — God's great love for us laying an obligation on us to love one another (the love received obliging love given); the 'so' pointing back to the greatness of God's love just described (vv.9-10), love at the cost of his Son, love to the unworthy, such love the ground and measure of our obligation; the proper response to being so loved being to love our fellow-believers (the love of God received must flow out in love to others, not to love being to despise the love we have received) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Beloved, if God so loved us, we ought also to love one another
the obligation to love one another, since God so loved us
God's great love laying an obligation to love
the love received flowing out in love given

schools.summary: The invisible God made known through love: 'no man hath seen God at any time (God in his essence invisible, never directly perceived by human sight, echoing John 1:18). If we love one another, God dwelleth (abides) in us, and his love is perfected (teleioutai — brought to completion, full expression) in us' — though God is invisible, his presence known and made real through love (when believers love one another, the unseen God dwelling in them, love the evidence and effect of his indwelling); and God's love reaching its goal and full expression in us when we love one another (the love God has shown us completed and made perfect as it flows out in our love to others, mutual love the perfecting of God's love in the believer) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Augustine: God, though unseen, is made present and known in us by love; when we love one another, the invisible God dwells in us, and his love is perfected — brought to its full end — in us; love is the way the unseen God becomes manifest, his own love completing itself as it passes through us to the brethren.

themes: No man hath seen God at any time. If we love one another, God dwelleth in us
and his love is perfected in us
the unseen God dwells in us when we love, his love perfected
mutual love the dwelling-place and completion of God's love

schools.summary: The witness of the Spirit: 'hereby (by this) know we that we dwell in him, and he in us, because he hath given us of his Spirit' — the assurance of the mutual indwelling (we in God, God in us) resting on the gift of the Holy Spirit (the indwelling Spirit the evidence and seal of the union with God); the reciprocal indwelling (cf. 3:24) real and known by the Spirit's presence (believer and God dwelling in each other, this intimate communion attested by the Spirit); the giving of the Holy Spirit the ground by which we know God dwells in us (the Spirit's presence and operation in the heart the witness of the union, love, v.12, and the Spirit together attesting the indwelling) — Barnes, Robertson.

themes: Hereby know we that we dwell in him, and he in us, because he hath given us of his Spirit
the gift of the Spirit assures the mutual indwelling
the indwelling Spirit the witness and seal of the union
love and the Spirit together attesting the indwelling

schools.summary: The apostolic affirmation: 'and we have seen (with our own eyes) and do testify (bear witness) that the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world' — the apostles, eyewitnesses of Christ, bearing testimony to what they have seen (their witness grounded in firsthand experience, cf. 1:1-3); the great truth witnessed being that God the Father sent his Son to save the world (the mission of the Son as Saviour the substance of their witness); the Son 'the Saviour of the world (kosmos)' — not of a narrow group only but of the world, the breadth of the title marking the fullness of the salvation Christ brings, the love of God reaching to the world in the sending of the Saviour (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: we have seen and do testify that the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world
the apostolic testimony that the Father sent the Son as Saviour of the world
the eyewitness witness of the apostles
the breadth of the salvation Christ brings

schools.summary: The confession joined to indwelling: 'whosoever shall confess (homologese — openly acknowledge, profess) that Jesus is the Son of God, God dwelleth in him, and he in God' — the sincere confession of Jesus as the Son of God joined to the mutual indwelling (such confession, when genuine, from the heart, marking and accompanying the abiding of God in the believer); this confession not mere words but the heartfelt acknowledgment of Jesus as God's Son (the genuine confession springing from faith and the mark of the indwelling God, the confession against which the false spirits stand, vv.2-3); the one who truly confesses Christ having the reciprocal indwelling, the same union spoken of throughout (v.13, 3:24), faith's confession and the mutual abiding going together (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Whosoever shall confess that Jesus is the Son of God, God dwelleth in him, and he in God
confessing Jesus as the Son of God brings the mutual indwelling
the heartfelt confession the mark of the indwelling God
faith's confession and the mutual abiding inseparable

schools.summary: The believer's apprehension of God's love and the union through love: 'and we have known (come to know) and believed (trusted, rested in) the love that God hath to (toward) us. God is love; and he that dwelleth (abides) in love dwelleth in God, and God in him' — believers having both known and trusted God's love for them (knowledge and faith together laying hold of the love of God); 'God is love' the central truth restated (v.8), the very nature of God love itself, the foundation of all John says about love; and since God is love, to abide in love being to abide in God (love the element in which the believer and God dwell in each other, the one who lives in love living in God, for love is God's own nature) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Augustine: because God is love, the one who dwells in love dwells in God and God in him; love is the very dwelling-place of communion with God, so that to abide in love is to abide in God himself — the soul that loves is joined to God, who is love, and is the temple of his presence.

themes: we have known and believed the love that God hath to us. God is love
he that dwelleth in love dwelleth in God, and God in him
dwelling in love is dwelling in God, who is love
love the element of communion with God

schools.summary: The confidence love brings: 'herein is our love made perfect (brought to completion), that we may have boldness (parresia — confidence, freedom from dread) in the day of judgment: because as he is, so are we in this world' — love brought to maturity in the believer producing confidence for the judgment (the one in whom love is perfected facing the day of judgment without terror, perfected love casting out the dread of judgment, the ground of holy boldness before God); the ground of the boldness being that 'as he (Christ) is, so are we in this world' — the believer made like Christ even now in this present world, sharing Christ's character (especially his love) and standing in Christ's righteousness, so able to face judgment confidently, for he is as Christ is (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Augustine: love brought to perfection gives boldness for the day of judgment, for the believer in whom love is complete is conformed to Christ — 'as he is, so are we in this world'; sharing Christ's love and likeness even now, the soul perfected in love approaches the judgment not with fear but with confidence, resting in him to whom it is made like.

themes: Herein is our love made perfect, that we may have boldness in the day of judgment
because as he is, so are we in this world
love perfected, giving boldness in the day of judgment
the believer made like Christ facing judgment without dread

schools.summary: The incompatibility of love and slavish fear: 'there is no fear (dread, terror) in love; but perfect (mature, complete) love casteth out (drives out, expels) fear: because fear hath torment (kolasin — pain, anguish, punishment). He that feareth (with slavish dread) is not made perfect (matured, completed) in love' — love and slavish fear cannot coexist (mature love expelling the dread of God and of judgment, as love grows fear departing); the fear in view the tormenting dread of punishment, the slavish fear that anticipates wrath (painful, the opposite of the confidence love gives, the fear of the guilty before a judge); the presence of tormenting fear showing that love has not yet reached maturity (the measure of love the absence of slavish fear, though not excluding reverent, filial fear) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Cyril: perfect love drives out the slavish fear that carries torment; the one still gripped by dread of punishment has not yet been made perfect in love — for the love that is matured in the soul replaces the fear of the bondservant with the confidence of the child, casting out the anguish that fear brings.

themes: There is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear: because fear hath torment
He that feareth is not made perfect in love
perfect love casts out the fear that has torment
mature love replacing slavish dread with confidence

schools.summary: The source of love: 'we love him (or simply, we love), because he first (prior to and before us) loved us' — our love for God (and for the brethren) the response to and the fruit of God's prior love (he loved us first, and our love the echo of his, love beginning with God); God's love original and ours derivative ('he first loved us' meaning his love precedes, causes, and kindles ours, we could not love had he not first loved us, all love in the believer the reflection of God's antecedent love); the believer's love the grateful response to being first loved (the experience of God's love producing love in return, love answering love) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: We love him, because he first loved us
our love springs from God's prior love
God's love original and ours derivative
love answering love

schools.summary: The exposure of false profession: 'if a man say (claims), I love God, and hateth his brother, he is a liar: for he that loveth not his brother whom he hath seen, how can he love God whom he hath not seen?' — to profess love for God while hating one's brother a lie (the two incompatible, the hater of his brother who claims to love God self-contradicted); the argument from the seen to the unseen — the brother seen, near, and concrete, love for him the testable, tangible thing, failure to love the visible brother betraying the absence of love; if one does not love the brother he can see, he cannot truly love the God he cannot see (love for the unseen God proved by love for the seen brother, the visible love the test of the invisible) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: If a man say, I love God, and hateth his brother, he is a liar
how can he love God whom he hath not seen
love of God cannot coexist with hatred of the brother
love for the seen brother the test of love for the unseen God

schools.summary: The binding charge: 'and this commandment have we from him (from God, or Christ), That he who loveth God love his brother also' — God himself having commanded that love for him be joined to love for the brother (the two loves bound together by divine command); the joining not a human inference only but a command from God (cf. Mark 12:30-31, the two great commandments), the authority of God binding love of God and love of neighbour together so that they cannot be separated; the one who loves God bound, by God's own command, to love his brother (love for God necessarily including love for the brethren, the second flowing from and proving the first) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: this commandment have we from him, That he who loveth God love his brother also
the commandment binding love of God to love of the brother
the two loves bound inseparably by divine command
love for God necessarily including love for the brethren
